{
  "idea": "Systemic JWST Spectroscopy Pipeline for False-Positive-Restricted Exoplanet Habitability Retrieval",
  "idea_description": "This end-to-end framework combines TESS candidate vetting, ExoMiner-style deep learning validation, JWST transmission spectroscopy retrieval, comparative multi-planet systemic abiotic baselines, and calibrated habitability modeling. It processes transit-detection and vetting outputs to feed atmospherically characterized candidates into a Bayesian comparative biosignature retrieval that uses planets in the same system to define abiotic baselines. By explicitly accounting for stellar contamination, retrieval degeneracy, and multi-planet abiotic trends, the pipeline identifies terrestrial worlds with biosignature-compatible atmospheres while avoiding false positives from abiotic or stellar explanations. It is calibrated against JWST CO2 detections and tested on validated TESS/Kepler candidates.",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "Biosignature attribution for low-SNR JWST exoplanet spectra contaminated by abiotic baselines, stellar contamination, or retrieval degeneracy.",
    "core_method": "A validated transit-candidate stream feeds into an explainable deep-learning vetting classifier, then into a systemic Bayesian retrieval comparing candidate atmospheres with empirical system abiotic baselines and biotic models.",
    "limitation_addressed": "False-positive biosignature attribution caused by abiotic baselines, stellar contamination, and retrieval model degeneracy.",
    "expected_result": "A JWST-era exoplanet candidate pipeline that identifies terrestrial planets with atmospheres consistent with biosignature-bearing worlds while rejecting abiotic and stellar false positives."
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": [
      "The TESS Objects of Interest Catalog from the TESS Prime Mission",
      "ExoMiner: A Highly Accurate and Explainable Deep Learning Classifier That Validates 301 New Exoplanets",
      "Identification of carbon dioxide in an exoplanet atmosphere"
    ],
    "reused_parts": [
      "TESS candidate cataloging and vetting",
      "ExoMiner-style explainable deep learning exoplanet validation",
      "JWST transmission spectroscopy retrieval for CO2 detection"
    ],
    "changed_parts": [
      "Adds systemic Bayesian retrieval comparing each candidate with empirical abiotic baselines from co-orbital planets",
      "Integrates uncertainty-calibrated habitability modeling after retrieval"
    ],
    "why_next_step": "JWST-era spectroscopy enables precise atmospheric characterization, but biosignature attribution remains blocked by false positives; the proposed framework turns candidate streams into calibrated abiotic-baseline retrieval pipelines.",
    "lineage_risk": "If TESS/Kepler candidates are not sufficiently vetted before spectroscopic follow-up, the abiotic system baseline may be poorly calibrated or contaminated by unvetted false positives."
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "Train and test the pipeline on validated Kepler and K2 candidates, then apply it to TESS Objects of Interest and JWST follow-up candidates, comparing retrieval anomalies against empirical system abiotic baselines.",
    "baselines": [
      "Single-planet atmospheric retrieval without system abiotic baselines",
      "ExoMiner validation-only vetting",
      "Prior discovery-era transit detection catalogs"
    ],
    "metrics": [
      "True positive rate for biosignature-compatible candidates",
      "False positive rate for abiotic, stellar-contaminated, and retrieval-degenerate cases",
      "Calibration of habitability model uncertainties"
    ],
    "risks": [
      "Insufficiently vetted candidate streams introduce false positives into the retrieval baseline",

prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Astrophysics / Exoplanet Science.
Below are 8 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
JWST-era spectroscopy and systemic-retrieval work make biosignature attribution the bottleneck: individual low-SNR spectra can be explained by abiotic baselines, stellar contamination, or model degeneracy. What end-to-end inference framework would combine transit detection, ML vetting, atmospheric retrieval, comparative multi-planet abiotic baselines, and uncertainty-calibrated habitability models to identify biosignature-bearing terrestrial worlds without false positives?

[Paper 1] The TESS Objects of Interest Catalog from the TESS Prime Mission
Brief Abstract: This paper presented the TESS Objects of Interest catalog from the prime mission, formalizing the large-scale candidate stream for the post-Kepler era. It provided the operational bridge from transit detection to community vetting and follow-up prioritization for bright nearby stars.

[Paper 2] ExoMiner: A Highly Accurate and Explainable Deep Learning Classifier That Validates 301 New Exoplanets
Brief Abstract: This NASA-led paper introduced ExoMiner, an explainable deep-learning system for exoplanet candidate validation, and used it to validate 301 new exoplanets from Kepler. It advanced ML vetting from candidate scoring toward robust, interpretable validation integrated with mission pipelines.

[Paper 3] Habitability and Biosignatures
Brief Abstract: This paper synthesized the emerging end-to-end agenda for life detection, linking exoplanet discovery, target vetting, atmospheric characterization, biosignature interpretation, and statistical assessment. It crystallized the next stage of the field: integrating observational pipelines with data-driven prioritization and habitability inference.

[Paper 4] Comparative biosignatures with systemic retrievals
Brief Abstract: Introduced a comparative biosignature framework based on systemic retrievals: a multi-planet Bayesian approach that uses planets in the same system to define an empirical abiotic baseline, then identifies potentially biological atmospheric anomalies by comparing abiotic and biotic explanatory models.

[Paper 5] Identifying Exoplanets with Deep Learning. II. Two New Super-Earths Uncovered by a Neural Network in K2 Data
Brief Abstract: This paper extended the deep-learning vetting paradigm from Kepler to K2 and used it to uncover two new super-Earths. It showed that ML-based transit vetting could generalize beyond the original Kepler pipeline context.

[Paper 6] Identification of carbon dioxide in an exoplanet atmosphere
Brief Abstract: Using JWST NIRSpec transmission spectroscopy, this paper reported the first clear detection of CO2 in the atmosphere of WASP-39b at 26-sigma significance. It marked a major transition from discovery-era pipelines to precision atmospheric characterization of transiting exoplanets.

[Paper 7] Planetary Candidates Observed by Kepler. III. Analysis of the First 16 Months of Data
Brief Abstract: This paper analyzed the first 16 months of Kepler data and identified 1,091 new viable planet candidates, bringing the total count to over 2,300. It established a statistical reliability framework for transit photometry catalogs, a prerequisite for scaling exoplanet discovery.

[Paper 8] Identifying Exoplanets with Deep Learning: A Five Planet Resonant Chain around Kepler-80 and an Eighth Planet around Kepler-90
Brief Abstract: This paper introduced a deep neural network to identify exoplanet transit signals in Kepler data. It demonstrated that machine learning could perform high-quality candidate vetting at scale and even recover previously missed planets, including an eighth planet around Kepler-90.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}

metadata.system_prompt: You are a scientific lineage analyst and idea generator. Respond with a single JSON object inside ```json ... ``` fences matching the schema requested in the user prompt. No commentary outside the JSON.
